title: Conclusions in a goodness-of-fit test
description: Make conclusions in a chi-square goodness-of-fit test based on the p-value and significance level.
topic_path: Chi-square test for goodness of fit

assessment_items.question_content:
A city has $4$ voting districts. Records from previous years show what proportion of residents live in each district. A political analyst wondered if those proportions still held true, so they took a random sample of $120$ residents. Here are their results along with a chi-square goodness-of-fit test:

District | A | B | C | D
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Hypothesized | $0.39$ | $0.27$ | $0.15$ | $0.19$
Observed | $58$ | $22$ | $12$ | $28$
Expected | $46.80$ | $32.40$ | $18.00$ | $22.80$
Components | $2.68$ | $3.34$ | $2.00$ | $1.19$
$\chi^2=9.20,\text{ DF}=3$ |  |  |  | 
$\text{P-value}=0.027$ |  |  |  | 

Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, what should the analyst conclude about the proportion of residents living in each district?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Hypotheses and conclusions

In a $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test, our null and alternative hypotheses generally look like this:

$\text{H}_0$: The hypothesized distribution is correct.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The hypothesized distribution is not correct.

We compare the P-value to the significance level to make a conclusion:

$\begin{align}
&\text{P-value }< \alpha \rightarrow \text{reject } \text{H}_0 \rightarrow \text{accept } \text{H}_\text{a} \\
&\text{P-value } \geq \alpha \rightarrow \text{fail to reject } \text{H}_0 \rightarrow \text{cannot accept } \text{H}_\text{a}
\end{align}$

###In this context

We could state the null and alternative hypotheses in this problem as:

$\text{H}_0$: The proportions from previous years are still true.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The proportions from previous years are no longer true.

Since the $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test produced a low P-value $(0.027<\alpha=0.05)$, we should reject $\text{H}_0$ and accept $\text{H}_\text{a}$.

###Answer

There is sufficient evidence to conclude that the proportions from previous years are no longer true.

choices.content: There is sufficient evidence to conclude that the proportions from previous years are no longer true.
We don't have enough evidence to conclude that the proportions from previous years are no longer true.
There is sufficient evidence to conclude that the proportions from previous years are still true.
No conclusions can be made from this test, because expected counts cannot include decimals. There must have been a mistake in the calculations.

assessment_items.question_content:
Salma operates a ramen restaurant. Historically, half of customers order pork ramen, and the other half of customers are split evenly between chicken and vegetarian ramen. Salma modified the chicken and vegetarian recipes, and she wonders if customers' ordering habits will change. Here are results from a sample of $32$ orders after launching the new recipes along with a chi-square goodness-of-fit test:

Type of ramen | Pork | Chicken | Vegetarian
- | -: | -: | -:
Historical distribution | $50\%$ | $25\%$ | $25\%$ 
Observed | $15$ | $4$ | $13$
Expected | $16$ | $8$ | $8$
Components | $0.06$ | $2$ | $3.13$
$\chi^2=5.19,\text{ DF}=2$ |  |  | 
$\text{P-value}=0.075$ |  |  | 

Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**At the $\alpha=0.10$ significance level, what should Salma conclude about the proportions of each type of order?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Hypotheses and conclusions

In a $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test, our null and alternative hypotheses generally look like this:

$\text{H}_0$: The hypothesized distribution is correct.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The hypothesized distribution is not correct.

We compare the P-value to the significance level to make a conclusion:

$\begin{align}
&\text{P-value }< \alpha \rightarrow \text{reject } \text{H}_0 \rightarrow \text{accept } \text{H}_\text{a} \\
&\text{P-value } \geq \alpha \rightarrow \text{fail to reject } \text{H}_0 \rightarrow \text{cannot accept } \text{H}_\text{a}
\end{align}$

###In this context

We could state the null and alternative hypotheses in this problem as:

$\text{H}_0$: The proportions of each type of order are the same as before Salma launched the new recipes.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The proportions of each type of order have changed since Salma launched the new recipes.

Since the $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test produced a low P-value $(0.075<\alpha=0.10)$, we should reject $\text{H}_0$ and accept $\text{H}_\text{a}$.

###Answer

There is sufficient evidence to conclude that the proportions of each type of order have changed.

choices.content: There is sufficient evidence to conclude that the proportions of each type of order have changed.
We don't have enough evidence to conclude that the proportions of each type of order have changed.
There is sufficient evidence to conclude that the proportions from before the new recipes are still true.
No conclusions can be made from this test, because one of the observed counts is too low for the calculations to be accurate.

assessment_items.question_content:
In basketball, a team can score from $3$-point shots, $2$-point shots, and $1$-point shots. Last season, a team recorded what type of shot each attempt was. The coach wonders if the attempts from the current season reflect the distribution from last season. Here are results from a sample of $40$ attempts from the current season along with a chi-square goodness-of-fit test:

Type of shot | $3$-points | $2$-points | $1$-point
- | -: | -: | -: 
Previous year's distribution | $31\%$ | $53\%$ | $16\%$ 
Observed | $9$ | $17$ | $14$
Expected | $12.4$ | $21.2$ | $6.4$ 
Components | $0.93$ | $0.83$ | $9.03$
$\chi^2=10.79,\text{ DF}=2$ |  |  |  
$\text{P-value}=0.005$ |  |  |  

Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**At the $\alpha=0.01$ significance level, what should the basketball coach conclude about the distribution of attempts?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Hypotheses and conclusions

In a $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test, our null and alternative hypotheses generally look like this:

$\text{H}_0$: The hypothesized distribution is correct.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The hypothesized distribution is not correct.

We compare the P-value to the significance level to make a conclusion:

$\begin{align}
&\text{P-value }< \alpha \rightarrow \text{reject } \text{H}_0 \rightarrow \text{accept } \text{H}_\text{a} \\
&\text{P-value } \geq \alpha \rightarrow \text{fail to reject } \text{H}_0 \rightarrow \text{cannot accept } \text{H}_\text{a}
\end{align}$

###In this context

We could state the null and alternative hypotheses in this problem as:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of attempts is the same as the previous year.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of attempts has changed.

Since the $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test produced a low P-value $(0.005<\alpha=0.01)$, we should reject $\text{H}_0$ and accept $\text{H}_\text{a}$.

###Answer

There is sufficient evidence to conclude that the distribution of attempts has changed.

choices.content: There is sufficient evidence to conclude that the distribution of attempts has changed.
We don't have enough evidence to conclude that the distribution of attempts has changed.
There is sufficient evidence to conclude that the distribution of attempts is the same as the previous year.
No conclusions can be made from this test, because expected counts cannot include decimals. There must have been a mistake in the calculations.

assessment_items.question_content:
The human resources department at a large company regularly conducts an employee satisfaction survey. One item on the survey presents employees with the statement, “I am happy overall at work.” Based on historical data, here is the distribution of responses to this item:

Response | Strongly agree | Agree | Disagree | Strongly disagree
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Historical distribution | $70\%$ | $15\%$ | $5\%$ | $10\%$

The human resources department wants to test if the most recent responses fit the historical distribution. Here is a sample of $150$ of  the most recent responses to the statement along with a chi-square goodness-of-fit test.

Response | Strongly agree | Agree | Disagree | Strongly disagree
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Observed| $104$ | $20$ | $14$ | $12$
Expected | $105$ | $22.5$ | $7.5$ | $15$
Components | $0.01$ | $0.28$ | $5.63$ | $0.6$
$\chi^2=6.52,\text{ DF}=3$ |  |  |  | 
$\text{P-value}=0.089$ |  |  |  | 

Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**At the $\alpha=0.10$ significance level, what should the human resources department conclude about the distribution of responses?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Hypotheses and conclusions

In a $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test, our null and alternative hypotheses generally look like this:

$\text{H}_0$: The hypothesized distribution is correct.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The hypothesized distribution is not correct.

We compare the P-value to the significance level to make a conclusion:

$\begin{align}
&\text{P-value }< \alpha \rightarrow \text{reject } \text{H}_0 \rightarrow \text{accept } \text{H}_\text{a} \\
&\text{P-value } \geq \alpha \rightarrow \text{fail to reject } \text{H}_0 \rightarrow \text{cannot accept } \text{H}_\text{a}
\end{align}$

###In this context

We could state the null and alternative hypotheses in this problem as:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of responses is the same now as it was historically.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of responses is different now than it was historically.

Since the $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test produced a low P-value $(0.089<\alpha=0.10)$, we should reject $\text{H}_0$ and accept $\text{H}_\text{a}$.

###Answer

There is sufficient evidence to conclude that the distribution of responses is different now than it was historically.

choices.content: There is sufficient evidence to conclude that the distribution of responses is different now than it was historically.
We don't have enough evidence to conclude that the distribution of responses is different now than it was historically.
There is sufficient evidence to conclude that the distribution of responses is the same as it was historically.
No conclusions can be made from this test, because expected counts cannot include decimals. There must have been a mistake in the calculations.

assessment_items.question_content:
When Silvia feeds her cat, she places food in $3$ different locations. She wondered if her cat was equally likely to eat first from each of the locations, so she tallied which location her cat chose first in a sample of $30$ feedings. Here are the results along with a chi-square goodness-of-fit test:

 Location| Kitchen | Bedroom | Living room
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Hypothesized | $0.333$ | $0.333$ | $0.333$
Observed | $13$ | $13$ | $4$
Expected | $10$ | $10$ | $10$
Components | $0.9$ | $0.9$ | $3.6$
$\chi^2=5.4,\text{ DF}=2$ |  |  |
$\text{P-value}=0.067$ |  |  |

Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, what should Silvia conclude about her cat's feeding preferences?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Hypotheses and conclusions

In a $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test, our null and alternative hypotheses generally look like this:

$\text{H}_0$: The hypothesized distribution is correct.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The hypothesized distribution is not correct.

We compare the P-value to the significance level to make a conclusion:

$\begin{align}
&\text{P-value }< \alpha \rightarrow \text{reject } \text{H}_0 \rightarrow \text{accept } \text{H}_\text{a} \\
&\text{P-value } \geq \alpha \rightarrow \text{fail to reject } \text{H}_0 \rightarrow \text{cannot accept } \text{H}_\text{a}
\end{align}$

###In this context

We could state the null and alternative hypotheses in this problem as:

$\text{H}_0$: Her cat is equally likely to eat first from each of the locations.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: Her cat is not equally likely to eat first from each of the locations.

Since the $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test produced a P-value that wasn't low enough $(0.067>\alpha=0.05)$, Silvia has failed to reject $\text{H}_0$. She doesn't have enough evidence to conclude $\text{H}_\text{a}$.

###Answer

There's not enough evidence to conclude that her cat prefers to eat first from some locations more than others.

choices.content: There is sufficient evidence to conclude that her cat prefers to eat first from some locations more than others.
There's not enough evidence to conclude that her cat prefers to eat first from some locations more than others.
There is sufficient evidence to conclude that her cat is equally likely to eat first from each of the locations.
No conclusions can be made from this test, because one of the observed counts is too low for the calculations to be accurate.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following table shows the approximate distribution of scores on the AP Latin exam in 2017:

Score | $5$ | $4$ | $3$ | $2$ | $1$
- | -: | -: | -: | -: | -:
Percent of test-takers | $12.5\%$ | $19.4\%$ | $31.5\%$ | $23\%$ | $13.6\%$

A group of Latin teachers gave a practice test to their students. The teachers were curious how well their scores on the practice test fit the 2017 score distribution, so they took a random sample of $50$ of the scores. Here are their results along with the results of a chi-square goodness-of-fit test:

Score | $5$ | $4$ | $3$ | $2$ | $1$
- | -: | -: | -: | -: | -:
Observed | $9$ | $13$ | $19$ | $5$ | $4$
Expected | $6.25$ | $9.7$ | $15.75$ | $11.5$ | $6.8$
Components | $1.21$ | $1.12$ | $0.67$ | $3.67$ | $1.15$
$\chi^2=7.83,\text{ DF}=4$ |  |  |  |  | 
$\text{P-value}=0.098$ |  |  |  |  | 

Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, what should the Latin teachers conclude about the distribution of their students' practice scores?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Hypotheses and conclusions

In a $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test, our null and alternative hypotheses generally look like this:

$\text{H}_0$: The hypothesized distribution is correct.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The hypothesized distribution is not correct.

We compare the P-value to the significance level to make a conclusion:

$\begin{align}
&\text{P-value }< \alpha \rightarrow \text{reject } \text{H}_0 \rightarrow \text{accept } \text{H}_\text{a} \\
&\text{P-value } \geq \alpha \rightarrow \text{fail to reject } \text{H}_0 \rightarrow \text{cannot accept } \text{H}_\text{a}
\end{align}$

###In this context

We could state the null and alternative hypotheses in this problem as:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of their students' practice scores is the same as the 2017 distribution.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of their students' practice scores is different from the 2017 distribution.

Since the $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test produced a P-value that wasn't low enough $(0.098>\alpha=0.05)$, the Latin teachers have failed to reject $\text{H}_0$. They don't have enough evidence to conclude $\text{H}_\text{a}$.

###Answer

There's not enough evidence to conclude that the distribution of their students' practice scores is different from the 2017 distribution.

choices.content: There is sufficient evidence to conclude that the distribution of their students' practice scores is different from the 2017 distribution.
There's not enough evidence to conclude that the distribution of their students' practice scores is different from the 2017 distribution.
There is sufficient evidence to conclude that the distribution of their students' practice scores is the same as the 2017 distribution.
No conclusions can be made from this test, because expected counts cannot include decimals. There must have been a mistake in the calculations.

assessment_items.question_content:
A forest is divided into $4$ regions by a road and a stream. A wildlife researcher was curious if the number of deer living in each region corresponded to the total area of each region. They used drones to take overhead images and counted how many deer were in each region. The following table shows the proportion of total area for each region of the forest and how many deer were observed in each region along with a chi-square goodness-of-fit test.

Region | A | B | C | D
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Land area distribution| $38\%$ | $30\%$ | $31\%$ | $1\%$
Observed deer | $408$ | $329$ | $330$ | $3$
Expected deer | $406.6$ | $321$ | $331.7$ | $10.7$
Components | $0.01$ | $0.2$ | $0.01$ | $5.54$
$\chi^2=5.75,\text{ DF}=3$ |  |  |  | 
$\text{P-value}=0.124$ |  |  |  | 

Assume that all conditions for inference were met and that the drone was equally likely to detect a deer's presence in any location.

**At the $\alpha=0.10$ significance level, what should the wildlife researcher conclude about the distribution of deer relative to the area?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Hypotheses and conclusions

In a $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test, our null and alternative hypotheses generally look like this:

$\text{H}_0$: The hypothesized distribution is correct.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The hypothesized distribution is not correct.

We compare the P-value to the significance level to make a conclusion:

$\begin{align}
&\text{P-value }< \alpha \rightarrow \text{reject } \text{H}_0 \rightarrow \text{accept } \text{H}_\text{a} \\
&\text{P-value } \geq \alpha \rightarrow \text{fail to reject } \text{H}_0 \rightarrow \text{cannot accept } \text{H}_\text{a}
\end{align}$

###In this context

We could state the null and alternative hypotheses in this problem as:

$\text{H}_0$: .  The distribution of deer matches the distribution of area.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of deer differs from the distribution of area.

Since the $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test produced a P-value that wasn't low enough $(0.124>\alpha=0.10)$, the wildlife researcher has failed to reject $\text{H}_0$. They doesn't have enough evidence to conclude $\text{H}_\text{a}$.

###Answer

There's not enough evidence to conclude that the distribution of deer differs from the distribution of area.

choices.content: There is sufficient evidence to conclude that the distribution of deer differs from the distribution of area.
There's not enough evidence to conclude that the distribution of deer differs from the distribution of area.
There is sufficient evidence to conclude that the distribution of deer matches the distribution of area.
No conclusions can be made from this test, because one of the observed counts is too low for the calculations to be accurate.

assessment_items.question_content:
An educational researcher wonders if students in their state are uniformly distributed across grades $5$ through $8$. Here are results from a random sample of $719$ students along with a chi-square goodness of fit:

Grade | $5^{\text{th}}$ | $6^{\text{th}}$ | $7^{\text{th}}$ | $8^{\text{th}}$
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Hypothesized | $25\%$ | $25\%$ | $25\%$ | $25\%$
Observed | $155$ | $211$ | $185$ | $168$
Expected | $179.75$ | $179.75$ | $179.75$ | $179.75$
Components | $3.41$ | $5.43$ | $0.15$ | $0.77$
$\chi^2=9.76,\text{ DF}=3$ |  |  |  | 
$\text{P-value}=0.021$ |  |  |  | 

Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**At the $\alpha=0.01$ significance level, what should the educational researcher conclude about the distribution of students among the $5^{\text{th}}$ through $8^{\text{th}}$ grades?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Hypotheses and conclusions

In a $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test, our null and alternative hypotheses generally look like this:

$\text{H}_0$: The hypothesized distribution is correct.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The hypothesized distribution is not correct.

We compare the P-value to the significance level to make a conclusion:

$\begin{align}
&\text{P-value }< \alpha \rightarrow \text{reject } \text{H}_0 \rightarrow \text{accept } \text{H}_\text{a} \\
&\text{P-value } \geq \alpha \rightarrow \text{fail to reject } \text{H}_0 \rightarrow \text{cannot accept } \text{H}_\text{a}
\end{align}$

###In this context

We could state the null and alternative hypotheses in this problem as:

$\text{H}_0$: The students are evenly distributed among the grades.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The students are not evenly distributed among the grades.

Since the $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test produced a P-value that wasn't low enough $(0.021>\alpha=0.01)$, the educational researcher has failed to reject $\text{H}_0$. They don't have enough evidence to conclude $\text{H}_\text{a}$.

###Answer

There's not enough evidence to conclude that  the students are not evenly distributed among the grades.

choices.content: There is sufficient evidence to conclude that  the students are not evenly distributed among the grades.
There's not enough evidence to conclude that  the students are not evenly distributed among the grades.
There is sufficient evidence to conclude that  the students are evenly distributed among the grades.
No conclusions can be made from this test, because expected counts cannot include decimals. There must have been a mistake in the calculations.

assessment_items.question_content:
A city has $4$ voting districts. Records from previous years show what proportion of residents live in each district. A political analyst wondered if those proportions still held true, so they took a random sample of $120$ residents. Here are their results along with a chi-square goodness-of-fit test:

District | A | B | C | D
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Hypothesized | $0.39$ | $0.27$ | $0.15$ | $0.19$
Observed | $58$ | $22$ | $12$ | $28$
Expected | $46.80$ | $32.40$ | $18.00$ | $22.80$
Components | $2.68$ | $3.34$ | $2.00$ | $1.19$
$\chi^2=9.20,\text{ DF}=3$ |  |  |  | 
$\text{P-value}=0.027$ |  |  |  | 

**Which voting district contributed the most to the test statistic?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Follow-up analysis of components

The terms that make up the sum of a $\chi^2$ test statistic are called **components.** We look for which components contributed the most to a test statistic because large components show instances where there was a large discrepancy between the observed and expected counts.

District | A | $\blueD{\text{B}}$ | C | D
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Hypothesized | $0.39$ | $0.27$ | $0.15$ | $0.19$
Observed | $58$ | $22$ | $12$ | $28$
Expected | $46.80$ | $32.40$ | $18.00$ | $22.80$
Components | $2.68$ | $\blueD{3.34}$ | $2.00$ | $1.19$
$\chi^2=9.20,\text{ DF}=3$ |  |  |  | 
$\text{P-value}=0.027$ |  |  |  | 

District $\blueD{\text{B}}$ has the largest component because its observed count was farthest away from its expected count (relative to the expected count). So we can say that District B contributed the most to the $\chi^2$ test-statistic.

###Answer

District B contributed the most to the test statistic.

choices.content: District A
District B
District C
District D

assessment_items.question_content:
Salma operates a ramen restaurant. Historically, half of customers order pork ramen, and the other half of customers are split evenly between chicken and vegetarian ramen. Salma modified the chicken and vegetarian recipes, and she wonders if customers' ordering habits will change. Here are results from a sample of $32$ orders after launching the new recipes along with a chi-square goodness-of-fit test:

Type of ramen | Pork | Chicken | Vegetarian
- | -: | -: | -:
Historical distribution | $50\%$ | $25\%$ | $25\%$ 
Observed | $15$ | $4$ | $13$
Expected | $16$ | $8$ | $8$
Components | $0.06$ | $2$ | $3.13$
$\chi^2=5.19,\text{ DF}=2$ |  |  | 
$\text{P-value}=0.075$ |  |  | 

**Which type of ramen contributed the most to the test statistic?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Follow-up analysis of components

The terms that make up the sum of a $\chi^2$ test statistic are called **components.** We look for which components contributed the most to a test statistic because large components show instances where the there was a large discrepancy between the observed and expected counts.

Type of ramen | Pork | Chicken | $\blueD{\text{Vegetarian}}$
- | -: | -: | -:
Historical distribution | $50\%$ | $25\%$ | $25\%$ 
Observed | $15$ | $4$ | $13$
Expected | $16$ | $8$ | $8$
Components | $0.06$ | $2$ | $\blueD{3.13}$
$\chi^2=5.19,\text{ DF}=2$ |  |  | 
$\text{P-value}=0.075$ |  |  | 

The $\blueD{\text{vegetarian}}$ ramen has the largest component because its observed count was farthest away from its expected count (relative to the expected count). So we can say that the vegetarian ramen contributed the most to the $\chi^2$ test-statistic.

###Answer

The vegetarian ramen contributed the most to the test statistic.

Chicken ramen
Vegetarian ramen

assessment_items.question_content:
The human resources department at a large company regularly conducts an employee satisfaction survey. One item on the survey presents employees with the statement, “I am happy overall at work.” Based on historical data, here is the distribution of responses to this item:

Response | Strongly agree | Agree | Disagree | Strongly disagree
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Historical distribution | $70\%$ | $15\%$ | $5\%$ | $10\%$

The human resources department wants to test if the most recent responses fit the historical distribution. Here is a sample of $150$ of  the most recent responses to the statement along with a chi-square goodness-of-fit test.

Response | Strongly agree | Agree | Disagree | Strongly disagree
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Observed| $104$ | $20$ | $14$ | $12$
Expected | $105$ | $22.5$ | $7.5$ | $15$
Components | $0.01$ | $0.28$ | $5.63$ | $0.6$
$\chi^2=6.52,\text{ DF}=3$ |  |  |  | 
$\text{P-value}=0.089$ |  |  |  | 

**Which response contributed the most to the test statistic?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Follow-up analysis of components

The terms that make up the sum of a $\chi^2$ test statistic are called **components.** We look for which components contributed the most to a test statistic because large components show instances where the there was a large discrepancy between the observed and expected counts.

Response | Strongly agree | Agree | $\blueD{\text{Disagree}}$ | Strongly disagree
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Observed| $104$ | $20$ | $14$ | $12$
Expected | $105$ | $22.5$ | $7.5$ | $15$
Components | $0.01$ | $0.28$ | $\blueD{5.63}$ | $0.6$
$\chi^2=6.52,\text{ DF}=3$ |  |  |  | 
$\text{P-value}=0.089$ |  |  |  | 

The response "$\blueD{\text{Disagree}}$" has the largest component because its observed count was farthest away from its expected count (relative to the expected count). So we can say that the response "Disagree" contributed the most to the $\chi^2$ test-statistic.

###Answer

The response "Disagree" contributed the most to the test statistic.

Agree
Disagree
